nativeRange.habitat: Found along rocky and coral reefs at depths of roughly 4–30 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented consuming sponges, sessile invertebrates, marine algae, and zooplankton in the wild. In aquariums, has been reported to accept spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations containing sponge, mysis shrimp, and other meaty foods. Feeding small portions two to three times daily is generally advised.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 7170x4780. Classic adult: bold dark-blue body, crisp white vertical band, blue-edged fins, and bright yellow caudal and pectoral fins - matches the described adult coloration exactly. Sharp, whole fish in frame.
LASZLO ILYES, 2786x1857. Tack-sharp, vivid transitional/subadult individual showing white band, yellow tail, yellow pectoral and orange-edged dorsal; extremely colorful. In-situ reef shot.
LASZLO ILYES, 3877x2585. Group/aggregation of multiple Passer Angelfish on the reef, adding natural-behavior variety; slightly softer/distant so kept as non-recommended.

summary: The Passer Angelfish is a large East Pacific species reaching up to 36 cm, generally considered not reef-safe, and best suited to experienced keepers with very large FOWLR systems of 1,000 L or more.
careNotes:
The Passer Angelfish, also known as the King Angelfish, is native to the Eastern Pacific, ranging from the Gulf of California south to Peru and including the Galapagos Islands. Adults tend to develop a bold dark-blue coloration with a distinctive white vertical band, a yellow-edged dorsal fin, and yellow caudal and pectoral fins. Juveniles are visually quite distinct, displaying orange and white or blue striping, and have been documented performing cleaning behavior on other fish — picking parasites from willing tankmates.

This species has been reported to reach around 35–36 cm in the wild, making it one of the larger angelfish kept in the aquarium hobby. A minimum tank of 1,000 L is generally advised, with many specialist sources recommending considerably more for long-term adult housing. The Passer is an active swimmer that benefits from ample open water, and tank length is considered more important than volume alone.

In the wild, Holacanthus passer tends to form monogamous pairs but does not defend exclusive territories. In captivity it has been known to be highly aggressive toward conspecifics and other angelfish species, and is generally best kept as the sole angelfish in a system unless the tank is exceptionally large. It tends to be semi-aggressive toward other large fish and may intimidate passive or smaller tankmates.

The Passer Angelfish is generally considered not reef-safe: it has been documented grazing on live rock and has been known to nip at stony and soft corals, clam mantles, and other sessile invertebrates. It is well suited to a large FOWLR (fish-only-with-live-rock) setup, where the absence of corals and delicate invertebrates removes the main compatibility concern.

Diet should include a variety of high-quality foods: sponge-based angelfish preparations, marine algae, spirulina, and meaty items such as mysis shrimp and krill. Feeding two to three times daily in small portions is generally advised.

Care is considered advanced given the large tank requirements, the species' sensitivity to water quality fluctuations, and its dietary need for sponge-based foods.
breedingNotes: Spawning in the wild has been documented from April through November, reportedly peaking around October–November near sunset. The species is a pelagic spawner, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. Captive breeding has not been reliably reported; successful reproduction in a home aquarium is considered essentially impractical given the tank sizes required and the species' behavior.

fish.breedingNotes: The Passer Angelfish is a pelagic spawner, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. Wild spawning has been documented from April through November. Captive breeding has not been reliably achieved.
